item.title: From Bestseller to Classic: What Makes a Book Endure?
item.name: From Bestseller to Classic: What Makes a Book Endure?
item.summary: Why do some books become classics? Two book buffs join us to explore why certain novels stand the test of time. Elizabeth Rosner is joined in lively conversation with veteran critic John McMurtrie (formerly of the San Francisco Chronicle ) about what makes certain novels resonate with readers over time.

copy.x: Tonight at Kepler's in Palo Alto: why do some books become classics while others fade? Elizabeth Rosner and critic John McMurtrie dig into that question at 6 PM. Worth the drive. https://www.keplers.org/upcoming-events-internal/classic-books
copy.threads: Tonight at Kepler's in Palo Alto, two people who've spent their lives thinking about books sit down to ask the big question: why do some novels last forever while others disappear? Elizabeth Rosner and veteran critic John McMurtrie (formerly of the SF Chronicle) get into it at 6 PM. Free, smart, and the kind of conversation you'll keep having on the drive home. 📚 https://www.keplers.org/upcoming-events-internal/classic-books #PaloAlto #BookLovers #SouthBay
copy.bluesky: Tonight at Kepler's in Palo Alto — Elizabeth Rosner and SF Chronicle critic John McMurtrie tackle the question: what actually makes a book a classic? 6 PM, and it's the kind of talk that'll rearrange your reading list. #BookLovers #PaloAlto
copy.facebook: If you love books and have ever wondered why certain novels stick around for centuries while others vanish, tonight at Kepler's in Palo Alto is worth your evening. Author Elizabeth Rosner sits down with veteran critic John McMurtrie — formerly of the San Francisco Chronicle — to dig into exactly that question. It starts at 6 PM and sounds like the kind of conversation that's genuinely hard to leave. https://www.keplers.org/upcoming-events-internal/classic-books
copy.instagram:
Tonight's pick for the South Bay 📚

Kepler's Books in Palo Alto is hosting one of those conversations that sounds simple on the surface — why do some books become classics? — but gets surprisingly deep once you're in the room.

Author Elizabeth Rosner is joined by John McMurtrie, a veteran literary critic who spent years at the San Francisco Chronicle, and together they're going to pull apart what makes certain novels resonate with readers across generations while others quietly disappear.

It starts at 6 PM. If you've ever finished a book and thought "this one's going to matter for a long time" — or picked up a so-called classic and wondered why anyone bothered — this is your crowd.

Kepler's has been running conversations like this for decades, and they tend to feel more like a great dinner party discussion than a lecture. Come with opinions.

https://www.keplers.org/upcoming-events-internal/classic-books

copy.instagram:
We planned your Tuesday — so you don't have to 🗓️

Here's how it goes:

☀️ Start at Zareen's in Palo Alto. One of the most-loved spots in the area, and a great way to kick off the day.

🌿 Head over to the Arastradero Creek Trail while the temperature is still mild. It's shaded, easy-going, and exactly what a Tuesday morning should feel like.

🎨 Swing through the Cantor Arts Center on Stanford's campus. The permanent collection alone is worth the stop — and it won't cost you anything.

🥙 Lunch at Dish N Dash on Stevens Creek in Cupertino. Go for the shawarma or build out a mezze spread. Solid Middle Eastern, every time.

🛝 Afternoon stop at Magical Bridge Playground in Palo Alto — one of the best all-abilities playgrounds in the Bay Area. Bring the kids or just take a walk through.

🍛 End the night at Shahi Darbar on El Camino in Santa Clara. Order the butter chicken, a biryani, and don't skip the bread. A warm, filling finish to a full day.

Full itinerary at https://southbaytoday.org/plan/9890070e

We planned your Sunday. Here's how it goes. ☀️

9:00 AM — Campbell Farmers Market (@campbellfarmersmarket). Local produce, artisan stuff, and something good to eat while you wander.

11:00 AM — Los Gatos Creek Trail. Flat, shaded, genuinely peaceful. A Sunday morning walk that actually feels like one.

12:30 PM — Luna Mexican Kitchen. Tacos and a margarita on the patio. Easy food, easy vibe, no complaints.

2:30 PM — Triton Museum of Art, Santa Clara. Rotating shows and a strong permanent collection of Bay Area work. More interesting than you might expect. 🎨

5:00 PM — Pairin Thai Massage. Book ahead and shake off the trail before dinner.

6:30 PM — Dio Deka, Los Gatos. Dress up just a little. Get a table on the patio. Order the Greek food. This is the right way to end a Sunday.

item.title: Draft Active Transportation Plan Public Workshop
item.summary: Community workshop to review the public draft of the Mountain View Active Transportation Plan and share input on creating a safer, more connected network for walking, biking, and rolling.

copy.x: Tonight in Mountain View: the city wants your input on how people walk, bike, and roll around town. 6 PM at City Hall. If you've ever wished a crosswalk existed or a bike lane connected somewhere useful — this is the room to say it. https://collaborate.mountainview.gov/atp
copy.threads: Tonight in Mountain View, the city is holding a public workshop on its Active Transportation Plan — basically, how do we make walking, biking, and rolling around town safer and more connected? 6 PM at City Hall. copy.facebook: Tonight in Mountain View — if you've ever thought "this intersection is dangerous" or "why is there no bike lane here," the city is literally asking for your opinion. The Active Transportation Plan public workshop is at City Hall starting at 6 PM. copy.instagram:
Tonight in Mountain View, the city is opening up its Active Transportation Plan for public input — and they genuinely want to hear from you. 🚶🚲

The workshop kicks off at 6 PM at Mountain View City Hall. The plan focuses on making it safer and easier to get around by foot, bike, or anything in between. If you've ever dodged a car in a crosswalk that felt too short, or wished a bike lane actually connected to something useful, this is where that frustration turns into something constructive.

Public workshops like this are how neighborhoods actually change. Show up, look at the draft plan, and tell them what's missing or what's working.

Details and more info at the link below 👇
https://collaborate.mountainview.gov/atp
